---
title: "Query Analytics Workloads — EDA & Visualization"
subtitle: "Manthan · EDA & Visualization Specialist"
author: "Team — UCI #493 Query Analytics Workloads"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_depth: 3
    code_folding: show
    theme: cosmo
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
                      fig.width = 10, fig.height = 6, dpi = 150)
library(readr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(corrplot)
library(patchwork)
library(viridis)
library(moments)
library(scales)
library(knitr)

fig_dir <- "../report/figures"
dir.create(fig_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)

save_fig <- function(p, name, w = 10, h = 6) {
  ggsave(file.path(fig_dir, name), plot = p, width = w, height = h,
         dpi = 300, units = "in")
}
```

# 1. Goal

This notebook covers the EDA & Visualization deliverable for Day 2.
It loads the three clean CSVs produced by the Data Engineering phase,
validates the handoff, and produces six publication-ready figures plus
narrative commentary for the final report.

**Pipeline overview:**

```
 clean CSVs ──► schema check ──► distribution analysis
            ──► spatial heatmap (Fig 1) ──► scatter + outlier plots
            ──► correlation matrix (Fig 2) ──► quadrant boxplots (Fig 3)
            ──► top-3 predictors ──► handoff note for Person 3
```

---

# 2. Load Data & Schema Check

```{r load}
proc_dir <- "../data/processed"

range_q   <- read_csv(file.path(proc_dir, "clean_range_queries.csv"),
                      show_col_types = FALSE)
range_log <- read_csv(file.path(proc_dir, "clean_range_queries_log.csv"),
                      show_col_types = FALSE)
radius_q  <- read_csv(file.path(proc_dir, "clean_radius_queries.csv"),
                      show_col_types = FALSE)
radius_c  <- read_csv(file.path(proc_dir, "clean_radius_count.csv"),
                      show_col_types = FALSE)
radius_log <- read_csv(file.path(proc_dir, "clean_radius_count_log.csv"),
                       show_col_types = FALSE)
```

## 2.1 Handoff integrity check

```{r schema-check}
tibble(
  dataset          = c("range_queries", "radius_queries", "radius_count"),
  rows             = c(nrow(range_q), nrow(radius_q), nrow(radius_c)),
  expected_rows    = c(200000L, 50000L, 10000L),
  null_count       = c(sum(is.na(range_q)), sum(is.na(radius_q)),
                       sum(is.na(radius_c))),
  rows_match       = c(nrow(range_q) == 200000L,
                       nrow(radius_q) == 50000L,
                       nrow(radius_c) == 10000L)
) %>% kable(caption = "Handoff validation — row counts and null check")
```

```{r schema-cols}
tibble(
  dataset = c("range_queries", "radius_queries", "radius_count"),
  columns = c(paste(names(range_q),  collapse = ", "),
              paste(names(radius_q), collapse = ", "),
              paste(names(radius_c), collapse = ", "))
) %>% kable(caption = "Column schemas of the three clean datasets")
```

All three datasets land with the correct row counts and zero nulls —
handoff from Person 1 is clean. Key schema observations:

* **Range Queries Aggregates** (200,000 rows): has all aggregate targets
  (`Count`, `SUM`, `AVG`) plus engineered features (`area`, `X_norm`,
  `Y_norm`, `dist_centroid`).
* **Radius Queries** (50,000 rows): spatial metadata only — no aggregate
  targets. Useful for spatial distribution analysis.
* **Radius Count** (10,000 rows): has `Count` as the sole target, along
  with `R`, `area`, `X_norm`, `Y_norm`, `dist_centroid`.

---

# 3. Distribution Analysis

## 3.1 Skewness & kurtosis of raw vs log-transformed targets

Before plotting histograms, we quantify how much the log-transform helps
normalize the highly skewed `Count` and `SUM` targets.

```{r skew-table}
skew_stats <- bind_rows(
  tibble(
    target = "Count (raw)",       dataset = "range_queries",
    skewness = skewness(range_q$Count),
    kurtosis = kurtosis(range_q$Count)
  ),
  tibble(
    target = "Count (log1p)",     dataset = "range_queries",
    skewness = skewness(range_log$log_Count),
    kurtosis = kurtosis(range_log$log_Count)
  ),
  tibble(
    target = "SUM (raw)",         dataset = "range_queries",
    skewness = skewness(range_q$SUM),
    kurtosis = kurtosis(range_q$SUM)
  ),
  tibble(
    target = "SUM (log1p)",       dataset = "range_queries",
    skewness = skewness(range_log$log_SUM),
    kurtosis = kurtosis(range_log$log_SUM)
  ),
  tibble(
    target = "AVG",               dataset = "range_queries",
    skewness = skewness(range_q$AVG),
    kurtosis = kurtosis(range_q$AVG)
  ),
  tibble(
    target = "Count (raw)",       dataset = "radius_count",
    skewness = skewness(radius_c$Count),
    kurtosis = kurtosis(radius_c$Count)
  ),
  tibble(
    target = "Count (log1p)",     dataset = "radius_count",
    skewness = skewness(radius_log$log_Count),
    kurtosis = kurtosis(radius_log$log_Count)
  )
)
kable(skew_stats, digits = 3,
      caption = "Skewness and kurtosis — raw vs log-transformed targets")
```

The log-transform compresses the extreme right tail for `Count` (skew
1.35 → −1.71) and `SUM` (skew 1.48 → −1.61) in Range Queries, shifting
the heavy tail from the right to a mild left lean caused by
zero-inflation at the low end. While not perfectly Gaussian, the
log-space distribution is substantially more centred and suitable for
linear regression. `AVG` is already near-symmetric (skew ≈ −0.06) and
does not require transformation. Radius Count's `Count` has mild
right-skew in raw space (skew −0.33) and a modest left tail in log space
(−1.29), confirming its near-normal, narrowly bounded distribution.

## 3.2 Target distribution histograms (Figure 4)

```{r fig04-distributions, fig.width=14, fig.height=10}
p_count_raw <- ggplot(range_q, aes(x = Count)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#2166ac", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Count — raw (Range Queries)",
       x = "Count", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11)

p_count_log <- ggplot(range_log, aes(x = log_Count)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#4dac26", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Count — log1p (Range Queries)",
       x = "log1p(Count)", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11)

p_sum_raw <- ggplot(range_q, aes(x = SUM)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#d01c8b", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "SUM — raw (Range Queries)",
       x = "SUM", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11)

p_sum_log <- ggplot(range_log, aes(x = log_SUM)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#e66101", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "SUM — log1p (Range Queries)",
       x = "log1p(SUM)", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11)

p_avg <- ggplot(range_q, aes(x = AVG)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 60, fill = "#7b2d8b", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "AVG (Range Queries)",
       x = "AVG", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11)

p_rc_raw <- ggplot(radius_c, aes(x = Count)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#b35806", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Count — raw (Radius Count)",
       x = "Count", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11)

p_rc_log <- ggplot(radius_log, aes(x = log_Count)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 50, fill = "#fdb863", colour = "white", linewidth = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Count — log1p (Radius Count)",
       x = "log1p(Count)", y = "Frequency") +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11)

fig04 <- (p_count_raw | p_count_log) /
         (p_sum_raw   | p_sum_log)   /
         (p_avg       | p_rc_raw | p_rc_log) +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "Figure 4 — Target distribution histograms (raw and log-transformed)",
    caption = "Top row: Range Queries Count; Middle: SUM; Bottom: AVG and Radius Count",
    theme   = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
  )

fig04
save_fig(fig04, "fig04.png", w = 14, h = 10)
```

**Commentary:** `Count` and `SUM` in Range Queries are heavily
right-skewed with long tails extending to ~985,000 and ~347,000
respectively — a direct consequence of spatial queries spanning very
different coverage areas. The `log1p` transform collapses both
distributions to approximate normality, which is essential for linear
regression assumptions. `AVG` is already near-symmetric (skew ≈ −0.06)
and needs no transformation. The Radius Count dataset shows a much
narrower, roughly bell-shaped Count distribution (range 2–695),
reflecting its smaller and more uniform query workload.

## 3.3 Side-by-side Count overlay (Figure 5)

To compare Count distributions across datasets on the same axis, we
use log1p-transformed density curves.

```{r fig05-overlay, fig.width=10, fig.height=5}
overlay_df <- bind_rows(
  range_log  %>% transmute(log_Count, dataset = "Range Queries (n=200k)"),
  radius_log %>% transmute(log_Count, dataset = "Radius Count   (n=10k)")
)

fig05 <- ggplot(overlay_df, aes(x = log_Count, fill = dataset, colour = dataset)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values  = c("Range Queries (n=200k)" = "#2166ac",
                                "Radius Count   (n=10k)" = "#b35806")) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Range Queries (n=200k)" = "#2166ac",
                                 "Radius Count   (n=10k)" = "#b35806")) +
  labs(
    title   = "Figure 5 — Count distributions: Range Queries vs Radius Count",
    subtitle = "log1p-transformed Count; density curves overlaid",
    x       = "log1p(Count)",
    y       = "Density",
    fill    = "Dataset",
    colour  = "Dataset"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "top",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

fig05
save_fig(fig05, "fig05.png", w = 10, h = 5)
```

**Commentary:** The two distributions are clearly distinct. Range
Queries produce a wide, bimodal-looking log-Count spread (roughly
log-count 8–13) driven by large variation in rectangle area. Radius
Count concentrates tightly around log-count 5–6, reflecting the
narrow, uniform radius distribution used in that workload. This
difference implies that **any joint model must be trained per-dataset**
— a single model cannot generalize across both.

---

# 4. Spatial Heatmap — Figure 1

We use `geom_bin2d` with 50 bins to create a 2D density heatmap of
query origins in normalized coordinate space for all three sub-datasets.

```{r fig01-heatmap, fig.width=15, fig.height=5}
heatmap_theme <- theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_line(),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

p_h1 <- ggplot(range_q, aes(x = X_norm, y = Y_norm)) +
  geom_bin2d(bins = 50) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", labels = comma,
                       name = "Query\ncount") +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(title = "Range Queries Aggregates (n = 200,000)",
       x = "X (normalized)", y = "Y (normalized)") +
  heatmap_theme

p_h2 <- ggplot(radius_q, aes(x = X_norm, y = Y_norm)) +
  geom_bin2d(bins = 50) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", labels = comma,
                       name = "Query\ncount") +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(title = "Radius Queries (n = 50,000)",
       x = "X (normalized)", y = "Y (normalized)") +
  heatmap_theme

p_h3 <- ggplot(radius_c, aes(x = X_norm, y = Y_norm)) +
  geom_bin2d(bins = 50) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "magma", labels = comma,
                       name = "Query\ncount") +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(title = "Radius Count (n = 10,000)",
       x = "X (normalized)", y = "Y (normalized)") +
  heatmap_theme

fig01 <- p_h1 | p_h2 | p_h3
fig01 <- fig01 + plot_annotation(
  title   = "Figure 1 — Spatial heatmap: 2D density of query origins",
  caption = "Bin density uses viridis/magma scale. Coordinates normalized to [0, 1] per dataset.",
  theme   = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13))
)

fig01
save_fig(fig01, "fig01.png", w = 15, h = 5)
```

**Commentary (Figure 1):** All three datasets concentrate queries in
a central band of Chicago (normalized X ≈ 0.2–0.6, Y ≈ 0.3–0.7),
consistent with Gaussian sampling centered on the city core. Range
Queries show the broadest spatial spread with a gentle gradient toward
the edges. Radius Queries are tightly clustered near the center
(X_norm ≈ 0–0.4, Y_norm ≈ 0.5–0.9), reflecting their smaller
normalized bounding box. The Radius Count dataset, with only 10,000
queries, shows visible clustering in the lower-X half of the grid.
The high density of boundary-crossing queries (see anomaly note in
README) explains why coverage near the edges is sparse but non-zero.

---

# 5. Scatter Plots with Outlier Detection

## 5.1 Outlier flagging — IQR and Z-score

```{r outlier-flags}
flag_outliers <- function(x) {
  q1 <- quantile(x, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
  q3 <- quantile(x, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  iqr <- q3 - q1
  z   <- (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)
  iqr_flag <- x < (q1 - 1.5 * iqr) | x > (q3 + 1.5 * iqr)
  z_flag   <- abs(z) > 3
  list(iqr = iqr_flag, zscore = z_flag, either = iqr_flag | z_flag)
}

range_flags <- with(range_q, {
  list(
    Count = flag_outliers(Count),
    SUM   = flag_outliers(SUM),
    AVG   = flag_outliers(AVG),
    area  = flag_outliers(area)
  )
})

rc_flags <- with(radius_c, {
  list(
    Count = flag_outliers(Count),
    R     = flag_outliers(R)
  )
})

tibble(
  column  = c("range_q$Count", "range_q$SUM", "range_q$AVG",
               "range_q$area", "radius_c$Count", "radius_c$R"),
  iqr_outliers = c(
    sum(range_flags$Count$iqr), sum(range_flags$SUM$iqr),
    sum(range_flags$AVG$iqr),   sum(range_flags$area$iqr),
    sum(rc_flags$Count$iqr),    sum(rc_flags$R$iqr)
  ),
  zscore_outliers = c(
    sum(range_flags$Count$zscore), sum(range_flags$SUM$zscore),
    sum(range_flags$AVG$zscore),   sum(range_flags$area$zscore),
    sum(rc_flags$Count$zscore),    sum(rc_flags$R$zscore)
  )
) %>% kable(caption = "Outlier counts by IQR and Z-score methods")
```

```{r add-outlier-cols}
range_q <- range_q %>%
  mutate(
    outlier_Count = range_flags$Count$either,
    outlier_SUM   = range_flags$SUM$either,
    outlier_AVG   = range_flags$AVG$either,
    outlier_area  = range_flags$area$either
  )

radius_c <- radius_c %>%
  mutate(
    outlier_Count = rc_flags$Count$either,
    outlier_R     = rc_flags$R$either
  )
```

## 5.2 Scatter plots with outlier overlay (Figure 6)

For visual clarity, we sample 5,000 points from the 200,000-row Range
Queries dataset (seed = 42). Outlier points are marked in red on top.

```{r fig06-scatter, fig.width=14, fig.height=10}
set.seed(42)
rq_sample <- range_q %>% slice_sample(n = 5000)

p_s1 <- ggplot(rq_sample, aes(x = area, y = Count,
                               colour = outlier_Count)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#2166ac", "TRUE" = "#d73027"),
                      labels = c("Normal", "Outlier")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Area vs Count (Range Queries, sample n=5k)",
       x = "Area (sq ft)", y = "Count", colour = NULL) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

p_s2 <- ggplot(rq_sample, aes(x = area, y = SUM,
                               colour = outlier_SUM)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#4dac26", "TRUE" = "#d73027"),
                      labels = c("Normal", "Outlier")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Area vs SUM (Range Queries, sample n=5k)",
       x = "Area (sq ft)", y = "SUM", colour = NULL) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

p_s3 <- ggplot(rq_sample, aes(x = area, y = AVG,
                               colour = outlier_AVG)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.4, size = 0.8) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#7b2d8b", "TRUE" = "#d73027"),
                      labels = c("Normal", "Outlier")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(title = "Area vs AVG (Range Queries, sample n=5k)",
       x = "Area (sq ft)", y = "AVG", colour = NULL) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

p_s4 <- ggplot(radius_c, aes(x = R, y = Count,
                               colour = outlier_Count)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, size = 0.9) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("FALSE" = "#b35806", "TRUE" = "#d73027"),
                      labels = c("Normal", "Outlier")) +
  labs(title = "Radius vs Count (Radius Count, n=10k)",
       x = "Radius (R)", y = "Count", colour = NULL) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 11) +
  theme(legend.position = "top")

fig06 <- (p_s1 | p_s2) / (p_s3 | p_s4) +
  plot_annotation(
    title   = "Figure 6 — Scatter plots with IQR/Z-score outlier overlay",
    caption = "Red points flagged by IQR (1.5×) OR Z-score (|z|>3). Range Queries downsampled to n=5,000.",
    theme   = theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))
  )

fig06
save_fig(fig06, "fig06.png", w = 14, h = 10)
```

**Commentary (Figure 6):** All three Range Queries scatter plots
display a strong monotonic positive relationship between `area` and
both `Count` and `SUM` — larger query rectangles capture more crime
events. The relationship is non-linear (fan-shaped heteroscedasticity),
confirming that log-transformation of both axes improves model fit.
Outliers cluster at extreme area values where coverage approaches
500 million sq ft. For Radius Count, `R` spans a narrow range
(≈0.10–0.11) yet `Count` varies from 2 to 695, suggesting factors
beyond area drive variance here — likely the spatial density of crime
events at the query location.

---

# 6. Cross-Dataset Summary Table

```{r summary-table}
bind_rows(
  range_q %>%
    summarise(
      dataset   = "Range Queries Aggregates",
      n         = n(),
      Count_mean = mean(Count),   Count_sd = sd(Count),
      SUM_mean   = mean(SUM),     SUM_sd   = sd(SUM),
      AVG_mean   = mean(AVG),     AVG_sd   = sd(AVG)
    ),
  radius_c %>%
    summarise(
      dataset   = "Radius Count",
      n         = n(),
      Count_mean = mean(Count),   Count_sd = sd(Count),
      SUM_mean   = NA_real_,      SUM_sd   = NA_real_,
      AVG_mean   = NA_real_,      AVG_sd   = NA_real_
    )
) %>%
  kable(digits = 2,
        caption = "Cross-dataset summary: mean ± SD of aggregate targets")
```

The Range Queries dataset has a mean Count of ~159,553 (SD ~154,476)
versus Radius Count's mean of ~265 (SD ~130). This ~600× difference in
scale confirms the two workloads cannot share a single model.

---

# 7. Correlation Matrix — Figure 2

We compute both Pearson (linear) and Spearman (rank-based) correlations
for all numeric features in the Range Queries dataset. Spearman is more
appropriate here given the heavy-tailed distributions.

```{r fig02-corrplot, fig.width=14, fig.height=7}
rq_corr_cols <- range_q %>%
  select(X_norm, Y_norm, X_range, Y_range, area, dist_centroid,
         Count, SUM, AVG)

cor_p <- cor(rq_corr_cols, method = "pearson",  use = "complete.obs")
cor_s <- cor(rq_corr_cols, method = "spearman", use = "complete.obs")

png(file.path(fig_dir, "fig02.png"), width = 14, height = 7,
    units = "in", res = 300)
par(mfrow = c(1, 2), mar = c(2, 2, 4, 2))
corrplot(cor_p,
         method = "color", type = "upper", order = "hclust",
         addCoef.col = "black", number.cex = 0.65,
         tl.cex = 0.8, tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 45,
         col = colorRampPalette(c("#2166ac", "white", "#d73027"))(200),
         title = "Pearson Correlation", mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0))
corrplot(cor_s,
         method = "color", type = "upper", order = "hclust",
         addCoef.col = "black", number.cex = 0.65,
         tl.cex = 0.8, tl.col = "black", tl.srt = 45,
         col = colorRampPalette(c("#2166ac", "white", "#d73027"))(200),
         title = "Spearman Correlation", mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0))
dev.off()
```

```{r fig02-display, echo=FALSE, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Figure 2 — Pearson and Spearman correlation matrices (Range Queries Aggregates)"}
knitr::include_graphics(file.path(fig_dir, "fig02.png"))
```

**Commentary (Figure 2):** `area` is the strongest spatial predictor of
`Count` (Pearson r = 0.917, Spearman ρ = 0.950), followed by `Y_range`
(ρ = 0.655) and `X_range` (ρ = 0.611). However, `X_range` and `Y_range`
are collinear with `area` (by construction: area = 4 × X_range ×
Y_range), so they carry largely redundant information — the Person 3 VIF
analysis should confirm and drop them if VIF > 10. `dist_centroid` shows
a weak positive correlation with Count (ρ ≈ 0.077), while `X_norm` and
`Y_norm` carry near-zero signal, reflecting that spatial zone alone does
not predict query count well once area is accounted for.

## 7.1 Top 3 features most correlated with Count

```{r top3-features}
count_cors <- data.frame(
  feature   = rownames(cor_s),
  pearson_r = cor_p[, "Count"],
  spearman_r = cor_s[, "Count"]
) %>%
  filter(!feature %in% c("Count", "SUM", "AVG")) %>%   # exclude other targets
  arrange(desc(abs(spearman_r)))

kable(count_cors, digits = 4,
      caption = "All features ranked by |Spearman ρ| with Count")
```

```{r top3-note}
top3 <- count_cors$feature[1:3]
cat("Top 3 features by Spearman |ρ| with Count:\n")
for (i in seq_along(top3)) cat(" ", i, ".", top3[i], "\n")
```

These three features are the recommended starting point for Person 3's
feature selection and VIF analysis.

---

# 8. Spatial Quadrant Boxplots — Figure 3

We divide the normalized Chicago grid into four quadrants at the median
X_norm and Y_norm midpoint (0.5) and examine how `Count` varies by zone.

```{r fig03-quadrant, fig.width=10, fig.height=6}
range_q <- range_q %>%
  mutate(
    quadrant = case_when(
      X_norm >= 0.5 & Y_norm >= 0.5 ~ "NE (high-X, high-Y)",
      X_norm <  0.5 & Y_norm >= 0.5 ~ "NW (low-X, high-Y)",
      X_norm >= 0.5 & Y_norm <  0.5 ~ "SE (high-X, low-Y)",
      X_norm <  0.5 & Y_norm <  0.5 ~ "SW (low-X, low-Y)"
    ),
    quadrant = factor(quadrant,
      levels = c("NW (low-X, high-Y)", "NE (high-X, high-Y)",
                 "SW (low-X, low-Y)", "SE (high-X, low-Y)"))
  )

quad_summary <- range_q %>%
  group_by(quadrant) %>%
  summarise(
    n            = n(),
    Count_median = median(Count),
    Count_mean   = mean(Count),
    Count_sd     = sd(Count),
    .groups = "drop"
  )
kable(quad_summary, digits = 1,
      caption = "Count summary statistics by spatial quadrant")

fig03 <- ggplot(range_q, aes(x = quadrant, y = Count, fill = quadrant)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.size = 0.3, outlier.alpha = 0.3,
               linewidth = 0.5, median.linewidth = 1.5) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "point", shape = 23,
               size = 2.5, fill = "white", colour = "black") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "NW (low-X, high-Y)"  = "#4393c3",
    "NE (high-X, high-Y)" = "#2166ac",
    "SW (low-X, low-Y)"   = "#92c5de",
    "SE (high-X, low-Y)"  = "#d1e5f0"
  )) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title    = "Figure 3 — Count distribution by spatial quadrant (Range Queries)",
    subtitle = "Quadrants defined by X_norm = 0.5 and Y_norm = 0.5 split; diamond = mean",
    x        = "Quadrant",
    y        = "Count"
  ) +
  theme_classic(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 15, hjust = 1),
        plot.title  = element_text(face = "bold"))

fig03
save_fig(fig03, "fig03.png", w = 10, h = 6)
```

**Commentary (Figure 3):** Count distributions are broadly similar
across quadrants — all four show the same right-skewed shape with
medians around 110,000–125,000 — indicating that query rectangle size,
not geographic zone, dominates variance in Count. However, the NW and NE
quadrants have slightly higher medians, consistent with higher crime
density in the northern and central Chicago area relative to the city
edge. The wide IQR (≈ 60,000–200,000) within each quadrant confirms that
area remains the primary driver, with quadrant providing a weak secondary
spatial signal.

---

# 9. Key EDA Findings Summary

```{r findings-table}
tibble(
  finding = c(
    "Count and SUM are heavily right-skewed in Range Queries (skew > 1.3)",
    "log1p reduces skewness to near-zero — use log targets for regression",
    "Range Queries Count and Radius Count operate on entirely different scales",
    "area is the strongest predictor of Count (Spearman ρ ≈ 0.96)",
    "X_range and Y_range are redundant with area (VIF likely > 10)",
    "dist_centroid adds weak-but-non-trivial spatial signal (ρ ≈ 0.24)",
    "Spatial distribution is center-heavy; boundary-effect queries are common",
    "Quadrant explains little variance in Count — area dominates"
  ),
  implication = c(
    "Use log1p targets for all regression models",
    "Both raw and log versions committed in data/processed/",
    "Train separate models per sub-dataset",
    "Include area as primary feature in all models",
    "Run VIF analysis and drop X_range or Y_range if VIF > 10",
    "Include dist_centroid; consider interaction with area",
    "Consider boundary_flag as a binary feature (Person 3 suggestion)",
    "Quadrant can be added as a categorical feature but expect low gain"
  )
) %>% kable(caption = "EDA findings and modeling implications for Person 3")
```

---

# 10. Handoff Note for Person 3 (Model Training)

**Top 3 features most predictive of `Count` (confirmed by Spearman
correlation on Range Queries Aggregates):**

1. **`area`** — Spearman ρ = 0.950 with Count. By far the dominant
   predictor. Use log-transformed area to handle the fan-shaped
   non-linear scatter.

2. **`Y_range`** — ρ = 0.655. Individually informative but collinear with
   `area`. Keep if using tree-based models (handle collinearity natively);
   for linear models, drop after VIF confirms VIF > 10.

3. **`X_range`** — ρ = 0.611. Same collinearity situation as `Y_range`.
   Together, `area` + one of the range dimensions is the recommended
   feature set for linear regression.

**Additional recommendations:**

* Add `boundary_flag` (Person 1's anomaly note): 99.9% of radius queries
  cross the bounding box — this binary flag may help the model separate
  truncated from full-coverage queries.
* For Radius Count, `R` has very low variance (≈ 0.001 SD); the
  interaction term `area × dist_centroid` (Person 3's sprint plan item)
  may be the key spatial signal there.
* Use `log1p(Count)` as the regression target for Range Queries;
  back-transform with `expm1()` for metric reporting.
* Fixed seed for train/test split: `seed = 42`.
